segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Listen to a talk in an archaeology class.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Imagine an ancient garden or field, and imagine organic material in its soil.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: things like plant roots or wooden irrigation pipes.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: When this organic material decays over the centuries, it often leaves behind voids in the soil.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Researchers can fill these voids by injecting plaster into the empty spaces.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: When the plaster hardens,

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: it creates casts,

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: essentially three-dimensional negatives that replicate what existed centuries ago.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: For example, at the ancient Maya village of Joya de Ceren in El Salvador, Central America.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: casts taken from root cavities have revealed a diverse mix of crops.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: including manioc, maize, and beans.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: These species were identified in part by the shape and size of the root voids.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: by comparing them with modern plant root systems.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: This layout of plants in the garden wasn't random at all.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: It showed the garden to be an early example of the milpa system.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: a traditional intercropping method still practiced in Central America and Mexico today, where

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: the tall maize plants provide stalks for bean plants to climb.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: the beans enrich the soil with nitrogen.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: and squash plants growing along the ground provide shade that suppresses weeds.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: and retains moisture.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Soil casts have also been used at the sites of ancient Roman villa

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: gardens, revealing irrigation channels and beds planted with species such as grapevines and roses.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: The findings show a deliberate integration of ornamental plants with edible plants.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: suggesting a Roman design ethos that sought to balance aesthetics with utility.
